statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description We call a set of integers $ Q $ a set of complementary sums if it can be obtained through the following actions: - choose an array $ a $ consisting of $ m $ positive integers ( $ m $ is any positive integer); - calculate the sum $ s $ of all elements in the array $ a $ ; - for each element $ a_{i} $ in the array, add the number $ s - a_{i} $ to the set, more formally the set $ Q = \{s - a_{i}\;|\; 1 \le i \le m\} $ . Note that $ Q $ is not a multiset, meaning each number in it is unique. For example, if the array $ a = [1, 3, 3, 7] $ was chosen, then $ s = 14 $ and $ Q = \{7, 11, 13\} $ . Your task is to count the number of distinct sets of complementary sums for which the following holds: - the set contains exactly $ n $ elements; - each element of the set is an integer from $ 1 $ to $ x $ . Two sets are considered different if there exists an element in the first set that is not included in the second set. Since the answer can be huge, output it modulo $ 998\,244\,353 $ . Input Format Each test consists of several test cases. The first line contains a single integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^{4} $ ) — the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows. The only line of each test case contains two integers $ n $ and $ x $ ( $ 1 \le n, x \le 2 \cdot 10^{5} $ ). Additional constraints on the input data: - the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 2 \cdot 10^{5} $ ; - the sum of $ x $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 2 \cdot 10^{5} $ . Output Format For each test case, output a single integer — the answer to the problem modulo $ 998\,244\,353 $ . Examples Sample 1 Input 5 1 7 2 5 3 10 27 31415 1000 999 Output 7 10 34 605089068 0 Note For the first test case, there are exactly $ 7 $ suitable sets: $ $$$\{1\}, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{4\}, \{5\}, \{6\}, \{7\} $ $

For the second test case, there are $ 10 $ suitable sets:

$ $ \{1, 2\}, \{1, 3\}, \{1, 4\}, \{1, 5\}, \{2, 3\}, \{2, 4\}, \{2, 5\}, \{3, 4\}, \{3, 5\}, \{4, 5\} $ $$$

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2125E - Sets of Complementary Sums
Let's first understand the following: what sets $$$Q$$$ can be obtained from some arrays $$$a$$$ using the operations described in the problem statement.
To do this, let's take an arbitrary set $$$Q$$$ and try to reconstruct any array from it. We know that the initial sum $$$s$$$ is at least $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$, because otherwise there would be a number $$$\le 0$$$ in the array.
Let's try to reconstruct some array with a sum exactly equal to $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$. We will create an empty array $$$a$$$ and then iterate through the elements of the set $$$Q$$$, adding to $$$a$$$ the numbers $$$\max(Q) + 1 - x$$$, where $$$x \in Q$$$. Okay, now let's calculate the sum of all elements in our $$$a$$$, and there can be three scenarios:
The sum equals the initial $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$; in this case, nothing needs to be done.
The sum is less than $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$; in this case, we need to add several copies of some number from those currently in $$$a$$$.
The sum is greater than $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$; in this case, the original set $$$Q$$$ cannot be obtained using the operations described in the problem statement.
For point two, it's easy: we notice that our $$$a$$$ contains the number $$$1$$$, so we can always make the sum of $$$a$$$ equal to $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$ simply by adding several ones.
Now let's consider the third point: we were unable to create the array, assuming its sum is $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$, but could the initial sum have been greater? Let's try to assume that the initial sum was $$$\max(Q) + 2$$$; however, we will again arrive at this third point. Let's try $$$\max(Q) + 3$$$, and it won't work either, nor will any subsequent value. Why is that?
Let's express the sum of our current $$$a$$$ under the assumption that it is $$$\max(Q) + 1$$$ in a more mathematical form:
$$$$$$\sum_{y \in Q} (\max(Q) + 1 - y) = (\max(Q) + 1) \cdot |Q| - \sum_{y \in Q} y$$$$$$
Now let's write the sum if the assumption was $$$\max(Q) + 2$$$:
$$$$$$(\max(Q) + 2) \cdot |Q| - \sum_{y \in Q} y$$$$$$
Subtract one from the other:
$$$$$$(\max(Q) + 2) \cdot |Q| - \sum_{y \in Q} y - ((\max(Q) + 1) \cdot |Q| - \sum_{y \in Q} y) = (\max(Q) + 2) \cdot |Q| - (\max(Q) + 1) \cdot |Q| = |Q|$$$$$$
It turns out that when we increased our assumption by $$$1$$$, the sum increased by the size of the set $$$Q$$$, and since the size of $$$Q$$$ is always at least one, we will never achieve equality between our assumption and the actual sum.
From this, we can conclude that for any set $$$Q$$$, a necessary and sufficient condition for it to be a
set of complementary sums
is:
$$$$$$(\max(Q) + 1) \cdot |Q| - \sum_{y \in Q} y \le (\max(Q) + 1)$$$$$$
or in a slightly different form:
$$$$$$(\max(Q) + 1) \cdot (|Q| - 1) - \sum_{y \in Q} y \le 0\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(1)$$$$$$
Now let's finally solve the problem itself. We would like to count such sets for which the above condition holds, and where $$$|Q| = n$$$ and $$$\max(Q) \le x$$$.
To do this, we start with the set $$$\{1, 2, ..., n\}$$$; from it, we can obtain other sets using one simple operation: adding $$$1$$$ to the suffix of the elements of the set.
For each such operation, we define $$$f(i)$$$ as the change in the sum on the left side of condition $$$(1)$$$ if we added $$$1$$$ to the suffix of length $$$i$$$. We can easily calculate all $$$f(i)$$$; they are equal to:
$$$$$$f(1) = n - 2$$$$$$ $$$$$$f(2) = n - 3$$$$$$ $$$$$$f(3) = n - 4$$$$$$ $$$$$$\dots$$$$$$ $$$$$$f(n - 1) = 0$$$$$$ $$$$$$f(n) = -1$$$$$$
Notice that the only operation that decreases the left side of condition $$$(1)$$$ is $$$f(n)$$$. This operation adds $$$1$$$ to all elements of the set $$$Q$$$.
So, we have the set $$$\{1, 2, ..., n\}$$$; it may violate condition $$$(1)$$$, and the only thing we can do is apply several operations of adding $$$1$$$ to all elements to satisfy equality in $$$(1)$$$. Let the number of such operations be $$$st$$$.
Now let's perform some operations in such a way that this equality is maintained. Suppose we added $$$1$$$ to the suffix of length $$$j$$$; to maintain equality, we need to perform $$$f(j)$$$ more additions of $$$1$$$ to all of $$$Q$$$, while the maximum in $$$Q$$$ increases by $$$f(j) + 1$$$.
All these considerations lead us to such an implementation: let $$$\text{dp}[i]$$$ be the number of different
sets of complementary sums
such that the maximum in these sets equals $$$i$$$ and equality in condition $$$(1)$$$ holds; the initial state is $$$\text{dp}[st + n] = 1$$$.
We will recalculate this dynamic programming as in the knapsack problem. We can represent the operations of adding to the suffix of length $$$j$$$ as an item with weight $$$f(j) + 1$$$ that we put into the knapsack:
for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++){
for (int j = 0; j < x; j++){
if (j + (i + 1) <= x){
dp[j + (i + 1)] = (dp[j + (i + 1)] + dp[j]) % MOD;
}
}
}
Thus, we will count the
sets of complementary sums
for which the inequality $$$(1)$$$ holds; all others can be obtained from those counted by performing $$$+1$$$ operations on the entire set. Specifically, we need to make one more pass over the dynamic programming, effectively turning it into a prefix sum array:
for (int i = 0; i < x; i++){
dp[i + 1] = (dp[i + 1] + dp[i]) % MOD;
}
After this, the answer will simply be the sum of the entire array $$$\text{dp}$$$.
There remains one problem: the current solution works in $$$O(n \cdot x)$$$ and does not fit within the time limits.
To optimize our solution, let's take a closer look at this inequality $$$(1)$$$. If the sum of all elements in $$$Q$$$ is large, then theoretically $$$\max(Q)$$$ is large and $$$|Q|$$$ is also large. What is the maximum possible sum of the elements in $$$Q$$$?
If we take $$$Q = \{\max(Q) - n + 1, \max(Q) - n + 2, \dots, \max(Q)\}$$$, we can easily derive the formula:
$$$$$$\max \sum_{y \in Q} y = \max(Q) \cdot |Q| - \frac{|Q| \cdot (|Q| - 1)}{2}$$$$$$
Substituting into condition $$$(1)$$$:
$$$$$$(\max(Q) + 1) \cdot (|Q| - 1) - \max(Q) \cdot |Q| + \frac{|Q| \cdot (|Q| - 1)}{2} \le 0$$$$$$
From here, we can express the relationship:
$$$$$$\max(Q) \ge \frac{|Q| \cdot (|Q| - 1)}{2} + |Q| - 1$$$$$$
From this, we see that the maximum in $$$Q$$$ depends on the square of $$$|Q|$$$, which means that for large $$$n$$$, the answer is always $$$0$$$. In other cases, we can compute our dynamic programming in $$$O(x \cdot \sqrt{x})$$$.

solutions.title: Solution (FelixArg)
solutions.content:
#include
<
bits
/
stdc
++.
h
>
using
namespace
std
;
#define
int
long
long
const
int
MOD
=
998
'244'
353
;
void
solve
(){
int
n
,
x
;
cin
>>
n
>>
x
;
// need sum(mx + 1 - ai) <= mx + 1
// start with 1, 2, 3, ... n
// min sum + 1 is n + 1
if
(
n
==
1
){
cout
<<
x
<<
'\n'
;
return
;
}
int
s_cur
=
n
*
(
n
+
1
)
/
2
;
int
need
=
s_cur
-
(
n
+
1
);
// leads to n is O(sqrt(s))
if
(
n
+
need
>
x
){
cout
<<
0
<<
'\n'
;
return
;
}
// so we can do dp[sum] knapsack
vector
<int>
dp
(
x
+
1
);
dp
[
0
]
=
1
;
// for -1 and 0
for
(
int
j
=
0
;
j
<
2
;
j
++){
for
(
int
i
=
0
;
i
<
x
;
i
++){
dp
[
i
+
1
]
=
(
dp
[
i
+
1
]
+
dp
[
i
])
%
MOD
;
}
}
for
(
int
i
=
1
;
i
<=
n
-
2
;
i
++){
for
(
int
j
=
0
;
j
<
x
;
j
++){
// increase sum by i -> mx increase by 1 + i
if
(
j
+
i
+
1
<=
x
){
dp
[
j
+
i
+
1
]
=
(
dp
[
j
+
i
+
1
]
+
dp
[
j
])
%
MOD
;
}
}
}
int
ans
=
0
;
for
(
int
i
=
0
;
i
<=
x
;
i
++){
int
cur
=
n
+
need
+
i
;
if
(
cur
>
x
){
break
;
}
ans
=
(
ans
+
dp
[
i
])
%
MOD
;
}
cout
<<
ans
<<
'\n'
;
}
signed
main
()
{
#ifdef
FELIX
auto
_clock_start
=
chrono
::
high_resolution_clock
::
now
();
#endif
ios_base
::
sync_with_stdio
(
false
);
cin
.
tie
(
0
);
cout
.
tie
(
0
);
int
t
=
1
;
cin
>>
t
;
while
(
t
--){
solve
();
}
#ifdef
FELIX
    cerr
<<
"Executed in "
<<
chrono
::
duration_cast
<
chrono
::
milliseconds
>(
chrono
::
high_resolution_clock
::
now
()
-
_clock_start
).
count
()
<<
"ms."
<<
endl
;
#endif
return
0
;
}
